coredata.dc:title: Study on the photothermal performance of supra-(carbon nanodots) developed with dicyandiamide N-doped
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Duan, Qianqian
Si, Si
Sang, Shengbo
Wang, Jialin
Zhang, Boye
Guan, Zhiwen
Jia, Muyue
Xue, Juanjuan
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, the dicyandiamide N-doped supra-CNDs were synthesized just within 24 h. The supra-CNDs exhibited a new strong absorption band at longer wavelengths (450–900 nm) and higher photothermal conversion efficiency (55.49 % and 46.02 % irradiation at 730 nm and 808 nm, respectively). The supra-CNDs can still maintain excellent photothermal stability and reusability after three on/off cycles. The survival rate of cancer cells incubated with supra-CNDs was above 90 %, but the value was drastically reduced to 4.24 % and 7.25 % after strong photothermal ablation at 730 nm and 808 nm, respectively. The cell killing ability of the supra-CNDs was also validated by photothermal ablation experiment. The excellent photothermal performance and low toxicity make the supra-CNDs highly promising application in photothermal therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Supra-carbon nanodots
Nanoparticles
Photothermal therapy
N-doped
Cancer
PTT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photothermal determination of supra-CNDs on cancer cells
Characterizations of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs
Uptake and cytotoxicity detection of supra-CNDs
Analysis of supra-CNDs for photothermal ablation of cancer cells

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400, 51622507 ), the Key Medical Scientific Research Projects in Shanxi Province (No. 2021XM12 ) and Open Fund from the Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology ( Shanxi Medical University ) (No. KLMEC/SXMU202001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b, c) HRTEM images of the blue CNDs. Inset: size distribution and FFT image of the blue CNDs. (d, e, f) HRTEM images of supra-CNDs. Inset: size distribution and FFT image of the supra-CNDs.

(a) The absorption spectra of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs, and the illustrations of blue CNDs (left) and supra-CNDs (right) without irradiation. (b) The fluorescence spectra of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs (initial concentration) excited at 340 nm. The illustrations of blue CNDs (left) and the supra-CNDs (right) excited 365 nm UV lamp.
(a) FTIR spectra of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs, (b) XPS of survey, (c) C1s, (d) N1s and (e) O1s peaks of high resolution XPS spectra of supra-CNDs.

Temperature evaluations of the supra-CNDs aqueous solution: different solution concentrations under 730 nm light irradiation (2 W/cm2) (a) and 808 nm light irradiation (2 W/cm2) (c); under different irradiation power density of 730 nm (b) and 808 nm light (d) with solution concentration of 1 mg/mL. (e) Infrared thermal images of the supra-CNDs aqueous solutions with different concentrations under the 730 nm or 808 nm light irradiation after 300 s. (f) Photothermal effect of the solution exposed to the laser irradiation at 2.5 W/cm2. The lasers were shut off after 300 s irradiation. (g) Plot of cooling time versus negative natural logarithm of the temperature driving force obtained from the cooling period after the laser irradiation. (h, i) Temperature variation of the supra-CNDs aqueous solution over three on/off irradiation cycles.

(a) Uptake of SMMC7721 cells incubated with supra-CNDs-FITC for different time. The scale bar is 25 μm. (b) Relative cell viability of SMMC7721 cells that were incubated with supra-CNDs aqueous solution at various concentration (0-1000 μg/mL). (c) Relative cell viability of SMMC7721 cells that were irradiated under 730 nm and 808 nm laser at power density of 2 W/cm2 for 5 min. (d) Cytotoxicity essay of the supra-CNDs against SMMC7721 cells after incubation for 24 h with irradiation under the 730 nm and 808 nm laser at power density of 2 W/cm2 for 5 min. (e) Cytotoxicity essay of the supra-CNDs against SMMC7721 cells after incubation for 24 h without irradiation and with irradiation under the 730 nm and 808 nm laser at power density of 2 W/cm2 for 5 min.

Fluorescence images of Calcein-AM/PI staining of SMMC7721 cells after incubation with different concentrations of supra-CNDs for 12 h and irradiation under 730 nm laser (a) and 808 nm laser (b) for 5 min at 2 W/cm2. Surviving cells are stained green, while dead cells are stained red. The scale bar is 100 μm.

head.ce:title: Study on the photothermal performance of supra-(carbon nanodots) developed with dicyandiamide N-doped

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Micro Nano Sensors & Artificial Intelligence Perception, College of Information and Computer, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanxi Key Laboratory of Micro Nano Sensors & Artificial Intelligence Perception, College of Information and Computer, Taiyuan University of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, the dicyandiamide N-doped supra-CNDs were synthesized just within 24 h. The supra-CNDs exhibited a new strong absorption band at longer wavelengths (450–900 nm) and higher photothermal conversion efficiency (55.49 % and 46.02 % irradiation at 730 nm and 808 nm, respectively). The supra-CNDs can still maintain excellent photothermal stability and reusability after three on/off cycles. The survival rate of cancer cells incubated with supra-CNDs was above 90 %, but the value was drastically reduced to 4.24 % and 7.25 % after strong photothermal ablation at 730 nm and 808 nm, respectively. The cell killing ability of the supra-CNDs was also validated by photothermal ablation experiment. The excellent photothermal performance and low toxicity make the supra-CNDs highly promising application in photothermal therapy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthetic procedure and action mechanism of supra-carbon nanodots.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to chemotherapy and radiotherapy, photothermal therapy (PTT) has been widely recognized as a minimally invasive treatment strategy to achieve the desired level of efficacy and avoid damage to normal tissues [1–5]. The core of PTT lies in the high-quality photothermal conversion probes as photosensitizers. An efficient PTT agent should exhibit strong absorbance in the near-infrared (NIR) range, high photothermal conversion efficiency and high system safety. Nowadays, the PTT agents have been derived from noble metal nanomaterials [6–11], metal and non-metal compounds [12–16] and organic dyes [17–19]. These agents usually exhibit strong absorbance in the NIR range and have a high photothermal conversion efficiency. However, high costs and poor biocompatibility of noble metal-based nanomaterials, non-degradability of metal and non-metal compounds as well as low photostability and poor hydrophilicity of conventional organic dyes limit their practical applications in the biological field.

Some new carbon nanomaterials have emerged in recent years for biological applications, such as carbon nanotubes [20,21] and carbon nanodots (CNDs) [22–24]. Carbon nanodots have emerged as an emerging carbon nanomaterial with excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties, which derived from surface states, molecular state, the conjugated π-domains of carbon core and the cross-link-enhanced emission (CEE) effect [25]. Moreover, its biocompatibility, low cost, and environmental friendliness make the CNDs consider to be a PTT agent with desirable properties. For example, Li et al. obtained supra-CNDs with strong absorption peaks and high photothermal conversion efficiency in the visible-NIR region, which were the self-assembly of CNDs with uneven surface charge distribution induced by moist air, but the synthesis time was long and prone to deliquescence under humid environment [26]. Zhang et al. developed supra-CNDs by pH-responsive self-assembly method, but the synthesis process requires strong acids and bases [22]. Although their experimentally obtained supra-CNDs have significant advantages over previously reported PTT agents, a facile, safe and rapid method for the synthesis of supra-CNDs has not been found.

It is well known that the aggregated state of nanoparticles can expand the absorption cross section and provide an additional absorption band in longer wavelengths, so it occurred to us that the small-sized CNDs can be used to aggregate into larger-sized supra-CNDs with near-infrared absorption. The larger-sized supra-CNDs can enter the cell efficiently and ensure that it is difficult to diffuse out of the cell and can guarantee a longer intracellular retention time. Recently, Geng et al. successfully achieved a high doping level of graphitic nitrogen in CNDs, which enabled CNDs to exhibit strong absorbance in the NIR region, prompting a significant increase in photothermal efficiency [24]. Inspired by the report, we tried to improve the photothermal efficiency of supra-CNDs through changing the doping level of graphitic nitrogen and form a simpler, faster and safer synthetic method.

Herein, the blue CNDs were prepared by a facile hydrothermal method [27], and then the supra-CNDs were synthesized by self-assembly of blue CNDs in moist air within 24 h. The synthesis parameters had been optimized to obtain the NIR absorbance bands of supra-CNDs ranging from 650 nm to 900 nm covered in the biological window. It is noteworthy that the graphitic nitrogen has a mark impact on the absorbance peak position of supra-CNDs. The photothermal conversion efficiency of supra-CNDs were studied by irradiation at 730 nm and 808 nm. The cell viability incubated with supra-CNDs was higher than 90 % showing its biocompatibility. The potential of supra-CNDs as a PTT agent was evaluated, and a substantial decrease in cancer cell viability was achieved under 730 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation. The excellent photothermal properties, low toxicity and high dispersibility in water make supra-CNDs a potential candidate for future biomedical applications.

Citric acid (CA) (≥99.5 %), dicyandiamide (≥98 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Taiyuan, China). Fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC) (97 %) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Thiazolyl blue tetrazolium bromide (MTT) (98 %) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Calcein acetoxymethyl ester (Calcein-AM) (≥96 %), and propidium iodide (PI) (≥94%) were purchased from BestBio Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was obtained from Tianhang Biochemical Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). High sugar medium (DMEM) was obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) and paraformaldehyde (4 %) were purchased from Beyotime (Shanghai, China). SMMC-7721 cells were purchased from Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences. Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments.

The absorption spectra were measured by a UV-vis-NIR spectrophotometer (UV-8000A, 190-1200 nm, Metash, China). High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images and fast Fourier transform (FFT) spot diagrams were recorded by a JEOL-2100F field emission TEM (JEOL, Hitachi, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra of samples were measured by FLS-980 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK). The surface functional groups of the samples were tested using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Tensor 27, Bruker Optik, Germany). The freeze-dried blue CNDs and supra-CNDs (1-2 mg) were mixed with KBr (near 200 mg) to prepare slices under the pressure to 8 MPa for infrared spectroscopy. In the photothermal experiments, an 808 nm CW laser (MDL-III-808 nm, CNI, China) and a 730 nm CW laser (MW-GX-730 nm, cclaser, China) were used as the irradiated sources, and the distance between the light source position and the aqueous surface of the quartz cuvette was set to 2.5 cm when it could provide the highest power density of 2.5 W/cm2. The irradiated area is about 1 cm2. The temperature changes of all samples were recorded with an infrared thermal camera (225 s, Fotric, China). Cell viability (the optical density value) was obtained by MTT assay using Microplate Reader (Thermo Scientific, Multiskan FC, China). Cell imaging results were carried out by fluorescence microscopy (Leica Microsystems, Leica DM 3000, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out by a PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Ulvac-Phi). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained using an XRD-7000 instrument with a Cu Kα radiation source. DLS measurements were performed in deionized water using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK), all samples were measured at 25 °C.

The synthesis of supra-CNDs refers to the work of Wu et al. [22] and Li et al. [26] with a slight improvement, and a safe, convenient and rapid preparation method is obtained. The details were listed in Table S1 in the Supporting Information. The blue CNDs were used as precursors, and further processed to obtain supra-CNDs. The synthesis steps of blue CNDs were as follows: 0.25 g citric acid and 1 g dicyandiamide were dissolved into 16 mL deionized water, then the solution was transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4 h. The aqueous solution was poured out while the Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave cooled to room temperature. Next, the aqueous solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 15 min to dislodge the non-fluorescent deposit, and the supernatant was slowly extracted at the end of centrifugation to obtain the blue CNDs aqueous solution for use (no other purification was done). The blue CNDs were then concentrated by freezing (-80 °C) and drying under vacuum. Using blue CNDs as raw materials, the supra-CNDs were further synthesized as follows: the freeze-dried yellow-green powder was exposed to a refrigerator with temperature set to 4 °C and humidity set to 60 % for 24 h, and the color of the CNDs powder gradually darkened under the action of the air. After 24 h, it was transferred to room temperature. The powder completely changed from yellow-green to dark green. After being dissolved in deionized water, a deep blue aqueous solution of supra-CNDs was obtained.

The supra-CNDs were diluted to various concentrations (0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, and 1.5 mg/mL, respectively), and poured each solution into a quartz cuvette in order to measure the photothermal performance of supra-CNDs, irradiated by 730 nm or 808 nm lasers with power density of 2.5 W/cm2 for 5 min. Then different laser power densities (1, 1.5, 2, and 2.5 W/cm2, respectively) were used to study the laser power dose effect. In order to observe the temperature change, we used an infrared camera to record the real-time temperature of the aqueous solution of supra-CNDs every 20 s. Using deionized water as a control, the photostability of the 1 mg/mL supra-CNDs aqueous solution was monitored by the temperature changes at different time points under the irradiation of 730 nm and 808 nm lasers at a power density of 2.5 W/cm2 to study its photostability. The photothermal conversion efficiency η was calculated of supra-CNDs according to formula (1).
(1)
η =
hA
T
max
−
T
surr
−
Q
Dis
I ( 1 − 1
0
− A λ
)

Among them, T
surr and T
max represent the ambient temperature and the maximum temperature, respectively. The absorbance Aλ of 1 mg/mL supra-CNDs aqueous solution at 730 nm and 808 nm are 2.762 and 1.118, respectively. The incident laser power density (I) at 730 nm and 808 nm are both 2.5 W/cm2. Q
Dis
(W) is the heat absorbed by the quartz cuvette itself, and the values measured at 730 nm and 808 nm are 0.088 W and 0.092 W under the same conditions, respectively (Q
Dis was obtained in independent experiment and the specific calculation process was added in the Supporting Information (Fig. S10)). Therefore, hA in formula (1) is unknown. Then, Eqs. (2) and (3) are introduced to obtain hA.
(2)
θ =
T −
T
surr
T
max
−
T
surr
(3)
τ
s
=
m
D
c
D
hA
m
D denotes the mass of the solution (2 g), c
D is the specific heat capacity of water (value of 4.2 J/g∙°C), and τ
s denotes the system time constant. According to Eq. (4), we can determine the time constant (τ
s ).
(4)
τ
s
= − T ( ln
θ
)
According to Eq. (3), hA can be determined.

Since the synthesized supra-CNDs have extremely faint fluorescent properties, in order to track the uptake of supra-CNDs by SMMC7721 cells, we used a green fluorescent derivative fluorescein isothiocyanate to label the supra-CNDs to obtain supra-CNDs-FITC, which showed green fluorescence by excitation at 488 nm [28,29]. SMMC7721 cells were selected with better morphology and in logarithmic growth phase, and inoculated into a 24-well plate containing sterile slides, and cultured at 37 °C, 5 % CO2. And then cells were continued to culture in the incubator for 24 h to make the cells stick to the slide. After washing the cells 3 times with PBS, the medium containing supra-CNDs-FITC was added to the well plate and continued the incubation. After culturing for 3 h, 6 h and 12 h, the well plates were taken out, the waste liquid was aspirated, and the slide was washed with PBS solution to remove dead cells and supra-CNDs-FITC that did not enter the cells. 1 mL of paraformaldehyde was taken to fix the cells for 15 min, then took out the climbing slide and placed it on a glass slide. Finally, the distribution of supra-CNDs-FITC in the cells was observed under a fluorescence microscope and recorded them to evaluate the uptake ability of SMMC7721 cells to supra-CNDs. The specific preparation and UV-vis-NIR spectra of the supra-CNDs-FITC were showed in Fig. S8 in the Supporting Information.

We evaluated the cytotoxicity of supra-CNDs by testing the activity of SMMC7721 cells incubated in an aqueous solution of supra-CNDs. After the cell culture was planted into a 96-well plate, the cells were regularly observed through a microscope. When the cell density in the well reached 80 % or more, the supra-CNDs solution with concentrations of 0, 100, 200, 400, 1000 μg/mL were added to the 96-well plate for continued co-incubation of supra-CNDs. After 24 h, MTT reagent (5 mg/mL) was added to each well, and then the 96-well plate was placed in the cell incubator. After continuing to incubate the cells for 4 h, a microplate reader was used to measure the absorbance of the solution in each well at 570 nm, and the cellular activity was calculated by Eq. (5):
(5) cell viability = (As - A0)/(A1 - A0)

In the above formula, As is the absorption value of experimental cells, A1 is the absorption value of untreated experimental cells in the control group, and A0 is the absorption value of blank cells. The effects of supra-CNDs on cytotoxicity and survival were evaluated by comparing the cell viability after incubation with different concentrations of supra-CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal determination of supra-CNDs on cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: The cell photothermal experiment of supra-CNDs was also based on SMMC7721 cells as the experimental cells. Two groups of 24-well plates were seeded with SMMC7721 cells, and both groups were added with supra-CNDs at concentrations of 0, 100, 200, 400, 1000 μg/mL. After 24 h cultivation, the two groups of cells were irradiated by 730 nm and 808 nm lasers (2 W/cm2) for 5 min, respectively. Calcein-AM/PI dyes were used to dissolve in the cell culture medium to stain the two groups of cells, and finally placed them under a laser scanning confocal microscope to perform fluorescence imaging and analysis of the cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: During the synthesis of supra-CNDs, we found that three synthesis parameters, the mass ratio of citric acid to dicyandiamine, temperature and time, had important effects on the peak of the NIR strong absorption band as well as the absorbance of the resulting supra-CNDs (Fig. S2). This is due to the fact that dicyandiamide plays three key roles in the hydrothermal process according to Wu et al.: as a precursor for N doping, a passivator, and providing a high-pressure basic reaction environment for the decomposition of dicyandiamide to NH3 at about 80 °C [27]. All these greatly facilitate the formation of N-doped blue CNDs. Then, the optimal conditions were found by further comparing the absorbant bands of supra-CNDs. The optimal conditions were found at a mass ratio of 1:4 between citric acid and dicyandiamide, temperature of 200 °C, and heating time of 4 h. Well-performing samples of blue CNDs were obtained under the optimized conditions and further processed to obtain supra-CNDs. Our final excellent sample was the one with the darkest blue color and the greatest fluorescence quenching obtained during the optimization process (Fig. S1). Besides, it was found that the peak redshift and absorbance of the NIR strong absorption band of the supra-CNDs may be related to the graphitic nitrogen during the optimization. Therefore, we performed XPS (Fig. S3) characterization of three samples with high absorbance (180 °C, 3 h; 200 °C, 3 h; 200 °C, 4 h, respectively) for further confirmation. All the next characterized supra-CNDs were optimized CNDs (200 °C, 4 h).

The HRTEM images of blue CNDs ( Fig. 1a) and supra-CNDs (Figs. 1d and S16) were shown in Fig. 1. The diameter of blue CNDs was about 3.78 nm on average and possessed well-resolved uniform lattice fringes (Fig. 1c). The 0.21 nm lattice spacing was consistent with the plane lattice spacing of graphene (100) (Fig. 1c and f) [30–32]. The Digital Micrograph was used to measure the lattice spacing (Figs. S13 and S14), the process of which was supplemented in the Supporting Information. The crystal lattice fringes of supra-CNDs (Fig. 1f) were adjacently located with the crystal lattice fringes of d
100 = 0.21 nm, inferring that they were the collectives of blue CNDs. The diameter of supra-CNDs was in the range of 10-60 nm. The results were further demonstrated by the FFT spot diagrams, which revealed the crystalline structure of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs [27]. To further prove the crystal phase of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs, the XRD patterns were performed (Fig. S4). The blue CNDs and the supra-CNDs showed a wide-angle XRD pattern with a broad peak corresponding to (002), which indicated the partial graphitization and small size of the blue CNDs and the supra-CNDs [31].

The UV-vis-NIR spectra of blue CNDs showed a separate absorption band at 240-400 nm ( Fig. 2a), which was consistent with previous literatures [32–35]. For the supra-CNDs, in addition to the absorption band at 240-400 nm, a broad and strong absorption band centered at 688 nm appeared, covering the biological window (Fig. 2a). The absorption band of the blue CNDs and the supra-CNDs at 240-350 nm were consistent with the π-π* transition of C=C, and the broad absorption at 340-400 nm were consistent with the n-π* transition of C=O [27,36]. The blue CNDs and the supra-CNDs had a broad absorption peak in the visible region, indicating that their surfaces had surface states that played an important role in their PL properties [36].

According to Fig. 2b, the blue CNDs showed bright blue fluorescence at 340 nm, whereas the supra-CNDs exhibited weak fluorescence due to the self-quenching effect [26]. The blue CNDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL properties, and the maximum emission intensity was obtained under 360 nm excitation (Fig. S11). The PL properties indicated that the luminescence of the blue CNDs were mainly from the strong contribution of surface related emission centers [37]. To further study the PL mechanism, the luminescence of blue CNDs prepared with different mass ratios of dicyandiamide to CA by hydrothermal method was compared (Fig. S12). The luminescence of blue CNDs enhanced with the increase of mass ratio, which indicated the strong PL emission of blue CNDs mainly came from the nitrogen atoms doped on the surface. Therefore, it could deduced that the PL emission of the blue CNDs came from the functional groups on the surface of the blue CNDs [27]. And the formation of the supra-CNDs from the blue CNDs was accompanied by a change of functional groups, which affected the PL properties of the supra-CNDs and led to the fluorescence quenching. In addition, the stability of the aqueous solution and the freeze-dried powder of supra-CNDs were tested by UV-vis-NIR spectra, in order to verify whether they could be stored for a long time (Fig. S9). There were slightly changes, indicating the enough stability of supra-CNDs for further use.

The surface properties of supra-CNDs were further characterized by FTIR spectra and XPS spectra in Fig. 3. Comparing the FTIR spectra of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs, the characteristic absorption peaks of the two are basically the same. The FTIR spectra of blue CNDs and supra-CNDs both show that there are three characteristic absorption bands. The absorption band of 1330-1500 cm-1 is derived from the role of C-H and C-N; the absorption peak at 1550-1800 cm-1 is mainly derived from the interaction of C=O and C=N. In addition, both the two types of CNDs have N-H (3190 cm-1) and O-H (3436 cm-1) structures [22]. Fig. 3b is the XPS total spectra of supra-CNDs. The analysis of the characteristic peaks of the energy spectra shows that supra-CNDs mainly contain three elements: carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen. Fig. 3c is the XPS spectra of C1s. The four characteristic peaks observed correspond to C=C (284.9 eV) [1,23], C-N/C-O (285.9 eV) [22,23], C=N/C=O (287.6 eV) [27,38] and O-C=O (289.1 eV) [39]; As Fig. 3d shows, there are three peaks at 398.5, 399.7 and 400.8 eV in the N1s spectra, which are attributed to pyridine-like nitrogen [40], pyrrole-like nitrogen [1,23] and graphitic nitrogen/N-H bands [32,40], respectively. Fig. 3e shows the XPS spectra of O1s with two characteristic peaks of C=O (531.5 eV) [1,27] and C-OH/C-O-C (533.1 eV) [27]. By comparing Fig. 3d with Figure S3, the graphitic N was the most abundant in supra-CNDs under optimal conditions, and it was also verified that the peak redshift and absorbance of supra-CNDs was related to the graphitic N. The specific parameters about the XPS spectra were listed in the Supporting Information (Tables S2–S7 and Figs. S17–S22).

The blue CNDs were also characterized by XPS spectra (Fig. S5). The XPS spectra of the two CNDs showed similar spectral profiles, specific discussion was added to the Supporting Information. In addition, we also measured the hydrodynamic size of blue CNDs (64.77 nm) and supra-CNDs (252.7 nm) by DLS (Fig. S6). Combining the results of HRTEM, UV-vis-NIR, PL, FTIR, XPS, DLS, etc., the supra-CNDs were the collectives of blue CNDs was proved.

The absorption spectra of supra-CNDs covers well the biological window NIR-I (650-900 nm), which makes them potential NIR photothermal properties. The supra-CNDs exhibit high absorption intensity in the visible-NIR region, but weak fluoresce emission, so it can be deduced that the energy is released as thermal energy. As shown in Fig. S15, the photothermal effect of supra-CNDs were verified by comparing the temperature of DI water, blue CNDs and supra-CNDs after 5 min of laser irradiation (808 nm, 2 W/cm2). The photothermal effect of supra-CNDs were further evaluated by measuring the temperature evaluations with different concentrations (0.1-1.5 mg/mL) or irradiation powers under continuous irradiation by 730 or 808 nm light. From Fig. 4a–d, it can be seen that there is a clear concentration and irradiation power dependence of the temperature increase of supra-CNDs, and the temperature can rapidly warm up to more than 30 °C within 5 min. Usually the temperature slightly higher than the physiological temperature (37 °C) of 40-45 °C is sufficient to kill cancer cells. And when the cells are at a temperature higher than 50 °C, it only take about 5 min to kill the cancer cells [41]. The infrared thermographic images presented in Fig. 4e show that the real-time temperature of 0.5 mg/mL of supra-CNDs solution was 51 °C after 5 min of laser irradiation at 730 nm (2 W/cm2), and the real-time temperature of 1 mg/mL of supra-CNDs solution was 52.7 °C after 5 min of laser irradiation at 808 nm (2 W/cm2). Therefore, we selected supra-CNDs with a concentration range of 0-1 mg/mL for the PTT experiments when the concentration as variable, and when the concentration was as a fixed parameter, the value was set as 1 mg/mL. These results demonstrate that the supra-CNDs can rapidly and efficiently convert light energy into heat via the photo-absorption at 730 or 808 nm, and have the potential to use as a PTT agent.

In order to measure the photothermal conversion efficiency at 730 nm and 808 nm, the temperature change of supra-CNDs under irradiation was monitored to determine the T
max and τ
s after being irradiated with a power of 2.5 W/cm2 for 300 s and then cooled down (Fig. 4f and g). The T
max and τ
s at 730 nm were 58.4 °C and 186.42 s, respectively. And the T
max and τ
s at 808 nm were 54.7 °C and 198.24 s, respectively. Based on these data, η
730 and η
808 were calculated as high as 55.49 % and 46.02 %, respectively. The supra-CNDs also presented excellent photothermal stability after 3 heating-cooling cycles, and no significant degradation was observed during the recovery process (Fig. 4h and i). In addition, we tested the optical properties of the aqueous supra-CNDs solution before and after laser irradiation, and the results showed that its vis-NIR absorption spectra remained basically unchanged before and after light irradiation (Fig. S7), which proved its high photothermal stability and indicated the potential of supra-CNDs as a long-lasting photothermal agent in PTT applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake and cytotoxicity detection of supra-CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake ability and cytotoxicity are important preconditions for the PTT agent application. The cellular uptake of supra-CNDs by SMMC7721 cells was evaluated visually by FITC as fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 5a, the cells exhibited bright green fluorescence, indicating the good uptake ability of the cells. It can be clearly seen that after 3 h of incubation, the supra-CNDs have entered the cytoplasm. The green fluorescence emitted by supra-CNDs-FITC became stronger with increasing incubation time. After 12 h, more and more supra-CNDs-FITC were taken up by SMMC7721 cells in the cytoplasm.

Then the cytotoxicity of supra-CNDs with different concentrations were estimated without irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5b, the survival rate of cancer cells was higher than 90 % in concentration rang of 0-1000 μg/mL, indicating no obvious biological toxicity of supra-CNDs. The low toxicity made the supra-CNDs benefit to reduce the damage to the normal cells. In addition, the cell survival rate of the blank groups (without supra-CNDs) were 91.6 % and 92.5 % after irradiating at 730 nm and 808 nm (2 W/cm2, 5 min), respectively, indicating that there was negligible damage to the cells by the laser irradiation at these parameters.

After the experimental group cells were irradiated with a 730/808 nm laser (2 W/cm2) for 5 min, the cell viability was significantly reduced (Fig. 5d and e), indicating the well killing capability of photothermal effect on the cancer cells. When the concentration of supra-CNDs was 1000 μg/mL, the survival rate of SMMC7721 cells was about 4.24 % and 7.25 % under 730 nm and 808 nm laser irradiation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of supra-CNDs for photothermal ablation of cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: Calcein-AM/PI staining was used to visually test the photothermal ablation effect. Before that, we first tested the damage of laser on SMMC7721 cells. The results showed that even though we exposed the cells to the laser and irradiated them for 5 min, some cells became round, but no cell death was observed ( Fig. 6). As shown in Fig. 6, the red fluorescence could be observed, which indicated that the death of cancer cells causing by the elevated temperature generated by supra-CNDs. As the concentration increased, more dead cells and rounded live cells could be observed. These results suggested that supra-CNDs had a significant photothermal ablation effect on SMMC7721 cells under laser irradiation. The visual analysis of photothermal ablation of cancer cells once again demonstrated that supra-CNDs can be used as a good PTT material and exhibited significant anti-cancer effects in vitro cell experiments.

In summary, the supra-CNDs which was prepared with a facile, fast, safe and low-cost method. The supra-CNDs exhibited broad UV-vis-NIR absorbance range (200-1000 nm). Excellent photothermal effect of supra-CNDs was obtained, and the photothermal conversion efficiency was 55.49 % and 46.02 % corresponding to 730 nm and 808 nm irradiation. Moreover, the supra-CNDs had excellent aqueous dispersibility and high photothermal stability. The biological toxicity of the supra-CNDs to SMMC7721 cells remained negligible until after the addition of light, demonstrating the excellent photothermal ablation effect of supra-CNDs on cancer cells. The excellent photothermal properties, strong visibility of NIR absorption and low toxicity enable the supra-CNDs for PTT. In addition, because of its ease of large-scale synthesis and functionalized modification, the supra-CNDs have potential to be combined with therapeutic drugs for the integration of chemotherapy and photothermal therapy, and used to further increase the killing rate of cancer cells.

Qianqian Duan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Si Si: Validation, Investigation, Experimental section, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Shengbo Sang: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Jialin Wang: Experimental section. Boye Zhang: Experimental section. Zhiwen Guan: Experimental section. Muyue Jia: Experimental section. Juanjuan Xue: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51975400, 51622507), the Key Medical Scientific Research Projects in Shanxi Province (No. 2021XM12) and Open Fund from the Key Laboratory of Cellular Physiology (Shanxi Medical University) (No. KLMEC/SXMU202001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129346.
